title: Contract Types: MSA, SOW, SaaS Terms, Engagement Letters

questions.question: What percentage of small business disputes arise from poorly defined scope?
questions.explanation: 60% of small business disputes arise from poorly defined scope — not bad intent or dishonest clients, but contracts that left too much to interpretation. The right contract type is the first line of defense.

questions.question: When should you use an MSA + SOW combination?

options.text: When you expect multiple projects or an ongoing engagement with the same client
When a handshake agreement isn't enough

questions.explanation: Use an MSA when you expect multiple projects with the same client. Sign the MSA once (covering liability, IP, confidentiality), then each new project gets a lightweight SOW with specific scope, timeline, and price.

questions.question: What does a Statement of Work (SOW) define that an MSA does not?

options.text: Project-specific scope, deliverables, timeline, and payment

questions.explanation: A SOW defines project-specific details: scope, deliverables, timeline, milestones, and payment. The broader relationship terms (liability, IP, confidentiality) come from the MSA it references.

questions.question: SaaS Terms of Service accepted by clicking 'I agree' are not legally binding.
questions.explanation: False. Click-wrap agreements (clicking 'I agree') are legally binding in all major jurisdictions. SaaS Terms of Service typically cover subscription terms, data handling, uptime SLAs, acceptable use, and termination.

questions.question: Based on your business model, which contract type(s) do you need? Do you currently use formal contracts? If not, what risks are you exposed to?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response correctly identifies the appropriate contract type for their business model (MSA+SOW for ongoing services, engagement letter for short projects, SaaS ToS for software), assesses their current contract situation honestly, and identifies specific risks from informal agreements.
